**County Durham**,[[note 1]](#cite_note-3) or **Durham**, is a [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_county "Ceremonial county") in [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England").[[3]](#cite_note-lieutenancies19972-4) The county borders [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland") and [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") to the north, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east, [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire") to the south, and [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria") to the west. The largest settlement is [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington").

The county has an area of 2,676 square kilometres (1,033 sq mi) and had an estimated population of 894,025 in 2024. The centre and east of the county are more densely populated than the west, and the major settlements include [Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool "Hartlepool") on the south-east coast, [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees") in the south-east, Darlington in the south, and the city of [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") in the north-centre. Stockton-on-Tees is part of the [Teesside](/wiki/Teesside "Teesside") conurbation, which extends into North Yorkshire. [Barnard Castle](/wiki/Barnard_Castle "Barnard Castle") is the largest town in the west of the county. For [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") purposes the county comprises the [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England") areas of [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham_(district) "County Durham (district)"), [Darlington](/wiki/Borough_of_Darlington "Borough of Darlington"), [Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool"), and part of [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees"). [Durham County Council](/wiki/Durham_County_Council "Durham County Council") is part of the [North East Combined Authority](/wiki/North_East_Combined_Authority "North East Combined Authority"), and the councils of the other three areas are part of the [Tees Valley Combined Authority](/wiki/Tees_Valley_Combined_Authority "Tees Valley Combined Authority"). The part of Tyne and Wear south of the [River Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") was historically part of County Durham, and the part of Durham south of the River Tees was historically part of the [North Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Riding_of_Yorkshire "North Riding of Yorkshire").

The west of the county contains part of the [North Pennines](/wiki/North_Pennines "North Pennines") uplands, a [national landscape](/wiki/National_Landscape "National Landscape"). The hills are the source of the rivers [Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees") and [Wear](/wiki/River_Wear "River Wear"), which flow east and form the valleys of [Teesdale](/wiki/Teesdale "Teesdale") and [Weardale](/wiki/Weardale "Weardale") respectively. The east of the county is flatter, and the two rivers meander through it; the Tees forms the boundary with North Yorkshire in its lower reaches, and the Wear exits the county near [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street") in the north-east. The [county's coast](/wiki/Durham_Coast "Durham Coast") is characterised by tall limestone and dolomite cliffs and is a [site of special scientific interest](/wiki/Site_of_special_scientific_interest "Site of special scientific interest").

What is now County Durham was on the border of [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), and contains survivals of this era at sites such as [Binchester Roman Fort](/wiki/Binchester_Roman_Fort "Binchester Roman Fort"). In the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") period the region was part of the [Kingdom of Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria"). In 995 the city of Durham was founded by monks seeking a place safe from [Viking](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age") raids to house the relics of St [Cuthbert](/wiki/Cuthbert "Cuthbert"). [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") was rebuilt after the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), and together with [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle") is now a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/Durham_Castle_and_Cathedral "Durham Castle and Cathedral"). By the [late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages") the county was [governed semi-independently](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham") by the [bishops of Durham](/wiki/Bishops_of_Durham "Bishops of Durham") and was also a buffer zone between England and Scotland. County Durham became heavily industrialised in the nineteenth century, when many [collieries](/wiki/Collieries "Collieries") opened on the [Durham coalfield](/wiki/Durham_coalfield "Durham coalfield"). The [Stockton and Darlington Railway](/wiki/Stockton_and_Darlington_Railway "Stockton and Darlington Railway"), the world's first public railway to use steam locomotives, opened in 1825.[[4]](#cite_note-_heritage2-5) Most collieries closed during the last quarter of the twentieth century, but the county's coal mining heritage is remembered in the annual [Durham Miners' Gala](/wiki/Durham_Miners%27_Gala "Durham Miners' Gala").

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The ceremonial county is officially named *Durham*,[[5]](#cite_note-lieutenancies-6) but the county has long been commonly known as *County* Durham and it is the only [English county](/wiki/English_county "English county") name prefixed with "County" in common usage (a practice common in [Ireland](/wiki/Counties_of_Ireland "Counties of Ireland")). Its unusual naming (for an English shire) is explained to some extent by the relationship with the [Bishops of Durham](/wiki/Bishops_of_Durham "Bishops of Durham"), who for centuries governed Durham as a [county palatine](/wiki/County_palatine "County palatine") (the [County Palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham")) outside the usual structure of county administration in England.

Early attestations of the county name include *Dunelmensischira* (Durhamshire) from c. 1100 and *Comitatus Dunelmensis* (County Durham) from 1344.[[6]](#cite_note-UNottsurvey-7)

The situation regarding the formal name in modern local government is less clear:

* The 2009 structural change legislation[[7]](#cite_note-cdsc2008-8) created the present [unitary council](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England") (that covers a large part – but not all – of the ceremonial county) refers to "the [county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") of County Durham" and names the new unitary [district](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district") "County Durham" too.
  + A later amendment to that legislation[[8]](#cite_note-9) refers to the "county of Durham" and the amendment allows for the unitary council to name itself "The Durham Council".
* The council retains the name as [Durham County Council](/wiki/Durham_County_Council "Durham County Council"). With either option, the name does not include *County* Durham.
* The [former postal county](/wiki/Former_postal_county "Former postal county") was named "County Durham" to distinguish it from the [post town](/wiki/Post_town "Post town") of Durham.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of County Durham](/wiki/History_of_County_Durham "History of County Durham")

### Anglian Kingdom of Bernicia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Anglian Kingdom of Bernicia")]

Main article: [Bernicia](/wiki/Bernicia "Bernicia")

Around 547 CE, an Angle named [Ida](/wiki/Ida_of_Bernicia "Ida of Bernicia") founded the kingdom of Bernicia after spotting the defensive potential of a large rock at Bamburgh, upon which many a fortification was thenceforth built.[[9]](#cite_note-Gething-10) Ida was able to forge, hold and consolidate the kingdom; although the native British tried to take back their land, the Angles triumphed and the kingdom endured.

### Kingdom of Northumbria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Kingdom of Northumbria")]

Main article: [Kingdom of Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria")

In 604 CE, Ida's grandson [Æthelfrith](/wiki/%C3%86thelfrith "Æthelfrith") forcibly merged Bernicia (ruled from [Bamburgh](/wiki/Bamburgh "Bamburgh")) and Deira (ruled from [York](/wiki/York "York"), which was known as *Eforwic* at the time) to create the [Kingdom of Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria"). In time, the realm was expanded, primarily through warfare and conquest; at its height, the kingdom stretched from the River Humber (from which the kingdom drew its name) to the Forth. Eventually, factional fighting and the rejuvenated strength of neighbouring kingdoms, most notably Mercia, led to Northumbria's decline.[[9]](#cite_note-Gething-10) The arrival of the Vikings hastened this decline, and the Scandinavian raiders eventually claimed the Deiran part of the kingdom in 867 (which became [Jórvík](/wiki/J%C3%B3rv%C3%ADk "Jórvík")). The land that would become County Durham now sat on the border with the [Great Heathen Army](/wiki/Great_Heathen_Army "Great Heathen Army"), a border which today still forms the boundaries between the historic counties of Yorkshire and County Durham.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert%27s_Church%2C_Chester-le-Street%2C_County_Durham.jpg/250px-St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert%27s_Church%2C_Chester-le-Street%2C_County_Durham.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert%27s_Church,_Chester-le-Street,_County_Durham.jpg)

The Church of [St Mary and St Cuthbert, Chester-le-Street](/wiki/St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert,_Chester-le-Street "St Mary and St Cuthbert, Chester-le-Street"), on the site of the wooden ninth century church built by the Lindisfarne monks

Viking settlement petered out at the Tees and the lands north thereof lay outside of the Danelaw. The House of Bamburgh, successors of the Kings of Bernicia and Northumbria, retained control north of the Tyne; between the Tyne and the Tees emerged the Community of St. Cuthbert or *Haliwerfolc*, successors of the See of Lindisfarne. The House of Wessex absorbed the Kingdom of York into the unified English Kingdom in 954 and claimed overlordship over Northumbria. However, the lands north of the Tees remained outside the governmental frontiers of the English realm, thus Bamburgh and the Haliwerfolc went on to become contested buffer states between England and Scotland.[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)

### City of Durham founded

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: City of Durham founded")]

Main article: [Durham, England](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England")

In 995 CE, St Cuthbert's community, who had been transporting Cuthbert's remains around, partly in an attempt to avoid them falling into the hands of Viking raiders, settled at Dunholm (Durham) on a site that was defensively favourable due to the horseshoe-like course of the River Wear.[[12]](#cite_note-13) St Cuthbert's remains were placed in a shrine in the White Church, which was originally a wooden structure but was eventually fortified into a stone building.

Once the City of Durham had been founded, the Bishops of Durham gradually acquired the lands that would become County Durham. Bishop Aldhun began this process by procuring land in the Tees and Wear valleys, including Norton, Stockton, Escomb and Aucklandshire in 1018. In 1031, [King Canute](/wiki/King_Canute "King Canute") gave Staindrop to the Bishops. This territory continued to expand, and was eventually given the status of a [liberty](/wiki/Liberty_(division) "Liberty (division)"). Under the control of the Bishops of Durham, the land had various names: the "Liberty of Durham", "Liberty of St Cuthbert's Land" "the lands of St Cuthbert between Tyne and Tees" or "the Liberty of Haliwerfolc" (holy [Wear](/wiki/River_Wear "River Wear") folk).[[13]](#cite_note-scammell-14)

The bishops' special jurisdiction rested on claims that King [Ecgfrith of Northumbria](/wiki/Ecgfrith_of_Northumbria "Ecgfrith of Northumbria") had granted a substantial territory to [St Cuthbert](/wiki/St_Cuthbert "St Cuthbert") on his election to the [see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see") of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") in 684. In about 883 a cathedral housing the saint's remains was established at [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street") and [Guthfrith, King of York](/wiki/Guthfrith,_King_of_York "Guthfrith, King of York") granted the community of St Cuthbert the area between the [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") and the [Wear](/wiki/River_Wear "River Wear"), before the community reached its final destination in 995, in Durham.

Following the [Norman invasion](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England"), the administrative machinery of government extended only slowly into northern England. Northumberland's first recorded [Sheriff](/wiki/High_Sheriff_of_Northumberland "High Sheriff of Northumberland") was Gilebert from 1076 until 1080 and a 12th-century record records Durham regarded as within the shire.[[14]](#cite_note-15) However the bishops disputed the authority of the sheriff of Northumberland and his officials, despite the second sheriff for example being the reputed slayer of [Malcolm Canmore](/wiki/Malcolm_Canmore "Malcolm Canmore"), King of Scots. The crown regarded Durham as falling within Northumberland until the late thirteenth century.

### County Palatine of Durham

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: County Palatine of Durham")]

Main article: [County Palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham")

[![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,8,54.7,-1.68,270x210.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=County_Durham&revid=1341872519&groups=_2c306e4b37e576ef1336498bcf5a24e52cf8a728&parser=legacy)](/wiki/Special:Map/8/54.7/-1.68/en)
Matters regarding the bishopric of Durham came to a head in 1293 when the bishop and his steward failed to attend proceedings of *[quo warranto](/wiki/Quo_warranto "Quo warranto")* held by the justices of Northumberland. The bishop's case went before parliament, where he stated that Durham lay outside the bounds of any English shire and that *"from time immemorial it had been widely known that the sheriff of Northumberland was not sheriff of Durham nor entered within that liberty as sheriff. . . nor made there proclamations or attachments"*.[[15]](#cite_note-16) The arguments appear to have prevailed, as by the fourteenth century Durham was accepted as a liberty which received royal mandates direct. In effect it was a private shire, with the bishop appointing his own sheriff.[[13]](#cite_note-scammell-14) The area eventually became known as the "[County Palatine](/wiki/County_Palatine "County Palatine") of Durham".

[Sadberge](/wiki/Sadberge_(wapentake) "Sadberge (wapentake)") was a liberty, sometimes referred to as a county, within Northumberland. In 1189 it was purchased for the see but continued with a separate [sheriff](/wiki/Sheriff "Sheriff"), [coroner](/wiki/Coroner "Coroner") and court of pleas. In the 14th century Sadberge was included in [Stockton](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees") ward and was itself divided into two wards. The division into the four wards of Chester-le-Street, [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington"), [Easington](/wiki/Easington_District "Easington District") and Stockton existed in the 13th century, each ward having its own coroner and a three-weekly court corresponding to the [hundred](/wiki/Hundred_(division) "Hundred (division)") court. The [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") was divided into the [archdeaconries](/wiki/Archdeacon "Archdeacon") of Durham and Northumberland. The former is mentioned in 1072, and in 1291 included the deaneries of Chester-le-Street, Auckland, [Lanchester](/wiki/Lanchester,_County_Durham "Lanchester, County Durham") and Darlington.

The term *palatinus* is applied to the bishop in 1293, and from the 13th century onwards the bishops frequently claimed the same rights in their lands as the king enjoyed in his kingdom.

### Early administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early administration")]

#### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Durham_-_John_Speed_Map.jpg/500px-Durham_-_John_Speed_Map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_-_John_Speed_Map.jpg)

The [historic boundaries](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") of the county shown in [John Speed](/wiki/John_Speed "John Speed")'s map of the county in his *Theatre of the Empire of Great Britaine*, c. 1611. These boundaries remained in use for administrative purposes until the local government reforms starting in the 1960s. A depiction of the city of Durham is inset in the top right.

The [historic](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") boundaries of County Durham included a main body covering the [catchment](/wiki/Catchment_area_(human_geography) "Catchment area (human geography)") of the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines") in the west, the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees") in the south, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") in the east and the Rivers [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") and [Derwent](/wiki/River_Derwent,_North_East_England "River Derwent, North East England") in the north.[[16]](#cite_note-vision_map-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) The county palatinate also had a number of [liberties](/wiki/Liberty_(division) "Liberty (division)"): the [Bedlingtonshire](/wiki/Bedlingtonshire "Bedlingtonshire"), [Islandshire](/wiki/Islandshire "Islandshire")[[18]](#cite_note-vision_islandshire-19) and [Norhamshire](/wiki/Norhamshire "Norhamshire")[[19]](#cite_note-vision_norhamshire-20) [exclaves](/wiki/Exclaves "Exclaves") within Northumberland, and the [Craikshire](/wiki/Craikshire "Craikshire") exclave within the North Riding of Yorkshire. In 1831 the county covered an area of 679,530 acres (2,750.0 km2)[[20]](#cite_note-vision_acres-21) and had a population of 253,910.[[21]](#cite_note-ns_durham-22) These exclaves were included as part of the county for parliamentary electoral purposes until 1832, and for judicial and local-government purposes until the coming into force of the [Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844](/wiki/Counties_(Detached_Parts)_Act_1844 "Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844"), which merged most remaining exclaves with their surrounding county. The boundaries of the county proper remained in use for administrative and ceremonial purposes until the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972").

#### Early English and Norman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early English and Norman period")]

Following the Battle of Hastings, [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") appointed Copsig as Earl of Northumbria, thereby bringing what would become County Durham under Copsig's control. Copsig was, just a few weeks later, killed in Newburn.[[22]](#cite_note-Dodds-23) Having already being previously offended by the appointment of a non-Northumbrian as Bishop of Durham in 1042, the people of the region became increasingly rebellious.[[22]](#cite_note-Dodds-23) In response, in January 1069, William despatched a large [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") army, under the command of [Robert de Comines](/wiki/Robert_de_Comines "Robert de Comines"), to [Durham City](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"). The army, believed to consist of 700 cavalry (about one-third of the number of Norman knights who had participated in the Battle of Hastings),[[22]](#cite_note-Dodds-23) entered the city, whereupon they were attacked, and defeated, by a Northumbrian assault force. The Northumbrians wiped out the entire Norman army, including Comines,[[22]](#cite_note-Dodds-23) all except for one survivor, who was allowed to take the news of this defeat back.

Following the Norman slaughter at the hands of the Northumbrians, resistance to Norman rule spread throughout Northern England, including a similar uprising in York.[[22]](#cite_note-Dodds-23) [William The Conqueror](/wiki/William_The_Conqueror "William The Conqueror") subsequently (and successfully) attempted to halt the northern rebellions by unleashing the notorious [Harrying of the North](/wiki/Harrying_of_the_North "Harrying of the North") (1069–1070).[[23]](#cite_note-24) Because William's main focus during the harrying was on Yorkshire,[[22]](#cite_note-Dodds-23) County Durham was largely spared the Harrying.[[24]](#cite_note-douglas-25) The best remains of the Norman period include [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") and [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle"), and several parish churches, such as St Laurence Church in [Pittington](/wiki/Pittington "Pittington"). The [Early English period](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") has left the eastern portion of the cathedral, the churches of Darlington, Hartlepool, and St Andrew, Auckland, Sedgefield, and portions of a few other churches.

#### Palatinate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Palatinate")]

Until the 15th century, the most important administrative officer in the [Palatinate](/wiki/Palatinate_of_Durham "Palatinate of Durham") was the [steward](/wiki/Steward_(office) "Steward (office)"). Other officers included the sheriff, the coroners, the [Chamberlain](/wiki/Chamberlain_(office) "Chamberlain (office)") and the [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor "Chancellor"). The palatine exchequer originated in the 12th century. The palatine assembly represented the whole county, and dealt chiefly with fiscal questions. The bishop's council, consisting of the clergy, the sheriff and the [barons](/wiki/Baron "Baron"), regulated judicial affairs, and later produced the [Chancery](/wiki/Court_of_equity "Court of equity") and the courts of [Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty_court "Admiralty court") and [Marshalsea](/wiki/Marshalsea_Court "Marshalsea Court").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Durham_Castle%2C_April_2017_%282%29_%2833802921566%29.jpg/250px-Durham_Castle%2C_April_2017_%282%29_%2833802921566%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Castle,_April_2017_(2)_(33802921566).jpg)

The entrance to [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle"), the bishops' palace until 1832 when it moved to Auckland Castle

The [prior](/wiki/Prior_(ecclesiastical) "Prior (ecclesiastical)") of Durham ranked first among the bishop's barons. He had his own court, and almost exclusive jurisdiction over his men. A UNESCO site describes the role of the bishops in Durham, the "buffer state between England and Scotland":[[25]](#cite_note-26)

> From 1075, the Bishop of Durham became a Prince-Bishop, with the right to raise an army, mint his own coins, and levy taxes. As long as he remained loyal to the king of England, he could govern as a virtually autonomous ruler, reaping the revenue from his territory, but also remaining mindful of his role of protecting England's northern frontier.

A report states that the Bishops also had the authority to appoint judges and barons and to offer pardons.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

There were ten palatinate barons in the 12th century, most importantly the [Hyltons](/wiki/Baron_Hylton "Baron Hylton") of [Hylton Castle](/wiki/Hylton_Castle "Hylton Castle"), the Bulmers of [Brancepeth](/wiki/Brancepeth,_County_Durham "Brancepeth, County Durham"), the Conyers of Sockburne, the Hansards of Evenwood, and the [Lumleys](/wiki/Earl_of_Scarbrough "Earl of Scarbrough") of [Lumley Castle](/wiki/Lumley_Castle "Lumley Castle"). The Nevilles owned large estates in the county. [John Neville, 3rd Baron Neville de Raby](/wiki/John_Neville,_3rd_Baron_Neville_de_Raby "John Neville, 3rd Baron Neville de Raby") rebuilt [Raby Castle](/wiki/Raby_Castle "Raby Castle"), their principal seat, in 1377.

[Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I "Edward I")'s *[quo warranto](/wiki/Quo_warranto "Quo warranto")* proceedings of 1293 showed twelve lords enjoying more or less extensive franchises under the bishop. The repeated efforts of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") to check the powers of the palatinate bishops culminated in 1536 in the [Jurisdiction in Liberties Act 1535](/wiki/Jurisdiction_in_Liberties_Act_1535 "Jurisdiction in Liberties Act 1535"), which deprived the bishop of the power to pardon offences against the law or to appoint judicial officers. Moreover, indictments and legal processes were in future to run in the name of the king, and offences to be described as against the peace of the king, rather than that of the bishop. In 1596 restrictions were imposed on the powers of the chancery, and in 1646 the palatinate was formally abolished. It was revived, however, after the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration"), and continued with much the same power until 5 July 1836, when the [Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836](/wiki/Durham_(County_Palatine)_Act_1836 "Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836") ([6 & 7 Will. 4](/wiki/6_%26_7_Will._4 "6 & 7 Will. 4"). c. 19) provided that the palatine jurisdiction should in future be vested in the Crown.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Dunelmensis_Atlas.jpg/250px-Dunelmensis_Atlas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dunelmensis_Atlas.jpg)

Hand-drawn map of Durham by Christopher Saxton in 1576

#### Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Wars")]

During the 15th-century [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses"), [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI_of_England "Henry VI of England") passed through Durham. On the outbreak of the [Great Rebellion](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") in 1642 Durham inclined to support the cause of [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"), and in 1640 the high sheriff of the palatinate guaranteed to supply the Scottish army with provisions during their stay in the county. In 1642 the [Earl of Newcastle](/wiki/William_Cavendish,_1st_Duke_of_Newcastle-upon-Tyne "William Cavendish, 1st Duke of Newcastle-upon-Tyne") formed the western counties into an association for the King's service, but in 1644 the palatinate was again overrun by a Scottish army, and after the [Battle of Marston Moor](/wiki/Battle_of_Marston_Moor "Battle of Marston Moor") (2 July 1644) fell entirely into the hands of Parliament.

#### Parliamentary representation and secular powers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Parliamentary representation and secular powers")]

In 1614, a [Bill](/wiki/Bill_(proposed_law) "Bill (proposed law)") was introduced in Parliament for securing representation to the county and city of Durham and the borough of [Barnard Castle](/wiki/Barnard_Castle "Barnard Castle"). The bishop strongly opposed the proposal as an infringement of his palatinate rights, and the county was first summoned to return members to Parliament in 1654. After the Restoration of 1660 the county and city returned two members each. In the wake of the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832") the county returned two members for two divisions, and the boroughs of [Gateshead](/wiki/Gateshead "Gateshead"), [South Shields](/wiki/South_Shields "South Shields") and [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland") acquired representation. The bishops lost their secular powers in 1836.[[28]](#cite_note-29) The boroughs of Darlington, Stockton and [Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool "Hartlepool") returned one member each from 1868 until the [Redistribution of Seats Act 1885](/wiki/Redistribution_of_Seats_Act_1885 "Redistribution of Seats Act 1885").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Urban

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Urban")]

The historic county excludes the village of [Startforth](/wiki/Startforth "Startforth"), the ceremonial county excludes the city of [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland") and the non-metropolitan county excludes the town of [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees"). The ceremonial county includes the non-metropolitan county with the addition of the boroughs of [Darlington](/wiki/Borough_of_Darlington "Borough of Darlington"), [Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool"), and [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees").[[5]](#cite_note-lieutenancies-6) The historic county's boundaries stretched from the rivers [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") to [Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees") and it is covered by the ceremonial county, the [city of Sunderland](/wiki/City_of_Sunderland "City of Sunderland"), the boroughs of [Gateshead](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Gateshead "Metropolitan Borough of Gateshead") and [South Tyneside](/wiki/South_Tyneside "South Tyneside"), while the non-metropolitan county includes part of [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire").

County Durham contains a small area of the [North East Green Belt](/wiki/North_East_Green_Belt "North East Green Belt") in the county's north; surrounding the city of Durham, Chester-le-Street and other communities along the shared county border with Tyne and Wear, avoiding [Wearside](/wiki/Wearside "Wearside")'s spread west. A smaller [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt_(United_Kingdom) "Green belt (United Kingdom)") separates [Urpeth](/wiki/Urpeth "Urpeth"), [Ouston](/wiki/Ouston,_County_Durham "Ouston, County Durham"), [Pelton](/wiki/Pelton,_County_Durham "Pelton, County Durham"), and [Perkinsville](/wiki/Perkinsville,_County_Durham "Perkinsville, County Durham") from [Birtley](/wiki/Birtley,_Tyne_and_Wear "Birtley, Tyne and Wear") in Tyne and Wear. A further small segment by the coast separates Seaham from the Sunderland settlements of Beckwith Green and [Ryhope](/wiki/Ryhope "Ryhope"). It was first drawn up in the 1990s.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

### Geology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Moorland_panorama_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1235001.jpg/960px-Moorland_panorama_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1235001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moorland_panorama_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1235001.jpg "The moors above Weardale")

The moors above [Weardale](/wiki/Weardale "Weardale")

County Durham is underlain by [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") rocks in the west. [Permian](/wiki/Permian "Permian") and [Triassic](/wiki/Triassic "Triassic") strata overlie these older rocks in the east. These [sedimentary](/wiki/Sedimentary "Sedimentary") sequences have been cut by [igneous](/wiki/Igneous "Igneous") [dykes](/wiki/Dyke_(geology) "Dyke (geology)") and [sills](/wiki/Sill_(geology) "Sill (geology)").[[30]](#cite_note-31)

The county contains a sizeable area of the [North Pennines](/wiki/North_Pennines "North Pennines"), designated an [Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty"), primarily west of [Tow Law](/wiki/Tow_Law "Tow Law") and [Barnard Castle](/wiki/Barnard_Castle "Barnard Castle").[[31]](#cite_note-32) The highest point ([county top](/wiki/List_of_counties_of_England_and_Wales_in_1964_by_highest_point "List of counties of England and Wales in 1964 by highest point")) of historic County Durham is the [trig point](/wiki/Trig_point "Trig point") (not the summit) of [Burnhope Seat](/wiki/Burnhope_Seat "Burnhope Seat"), height 746 metres (2,448 ft), between Weardale and Teesdale on the border with historic [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland "Cumberland") in the far west of the county. The local government reorganisation of 1974 placed the higher [Mickle Fell](/wiki/Mickle_Fell "Mickle Fell") south of Teesdale (the county top of [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire")) within the administrative borders of Durham (where it remains within the ceremonial county). However, it is not generally recognised as the highest point in Durham.

The two main dales of County Durham (Teesdale and Weardale) and the surrounding fells, many of which exceed 2,000 feet (610 m) in height, are excellent [hillwalking](/wiki/Hillwalking "Hillwalking") country, although not nearly as popular as the nearby [Yorkshire Dales](/wiki/Yorkshire_Dales "Yorkshire Dales") and [Lake District](/wiki/Lake_District "Lake District") national parks. The scenery is rugged and remote, and the high fells have a landscape typical of the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines") with extensive areas of [tussock grass](/wiki/Tussock_grass "Tussock grass") and blanket [peat bog](/wiki/Peat_bog "Peat bog") in the west, with [heather](/wiki/Ericaceae "Ericaceae") [moorland](/wiki/Moorland "Moorland") on the lower slopes descending to the east.

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Governance")]

Main article: [History of local government districts in Durham](/wiki/History_of_local_government_districts_in_Durham "History of local government districts in Durham")

County Durham, as considered a county for lieutenancy purposes by the Lieutenancies Act 1997, is administered as a part of the [constituent country](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") of England in the United Kingdom.[[3]](#cite_note-lieutenancies19972-4) The area is appointed a [lord lieutenant](/wiki/Lord_Lieutenant_of_Durham "Lord Lieutenant of Durham") and a [high sheriff](/wiki/High_Sheriff_of_Durham "High Sheriff of Durham").

The ceremonial county is divided into four districts (see table below), one of which – Stockton-on-Tees – also extends into North Yorkshire. For administrative purposes, the County of Durham only consists of the [area governed by Durham County Council](/wiki/County_Durham_(district) "County Durham (district)").[[38]](#cite_note-durham2008-39) The three other areas are counties in their own right.[[39]](#cite_note-durham1995-40)[[40]](#cite_note-cleveland1995-41)

| Area name | Council | Council headquarters | Established |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Durham | [Durham County Council](/wiki/Durham_County_Council "Durham County Council") | County Hall, Durham | 1 April 2009[[38]](#cite_note-durham2008-39) |
| Borough of Darlington | [Darlington Borough Council](/wiki/Borough_of_Darlington "Borough of Darlington") | Darlington Town Hall, Darlington | c. 1995[[39]](#cite_note-durham1995-40) |
| Stockton-on-Tees | [Stockton-on-Tees Borough Council](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees_Borough_Council "Stockton-on-Tees Borough Council") |  | c. 1995[[40]](#cite_note-cleveland1995-41) |
| Hartlepool | [Hartlepool Borough Council](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool") |  | c. 1995[[40]](#cite_note-cleveland1995-41) |

The county is partially [parished](/wiki/List_of_civil_parishes_in_County_Durham "List of civil parishes in County Durham"). The [city of Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") is the most populous settlement in the county to have a parish. Multiple parishes are styled as having [town councils](/wiki/Town_council "Town council"): [Billingham](/wiki/Billingham "Billingham") (in Stockton Borough), [Barnard Castle](/wiki/Barnard_Castle "Barnard Castle"), [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland "Bishop Auckland"), [Chilton](/wiki/Chilton,_County_Durham "Chilton, County Durham"), [Ferryhill](/wiki/Ferryhill "Ferryhill"), [Great Aycliffe](/wiki/Great_Aycliffe "Great Aycliffe"), [Newton Aycliffe](/wiki/Newton_Aycliffe "Newton Aycliffe"), [Greater Willington](/wiki/Greater_Willington "Greater Willington"), [Peterlee](/wiki/Peterlee "Peterlee"), [Seaham](/wiki/Seaham "Seaham"), [Sedgefield](/wiki/Sedgefield "Sedgefield"), [Shildon](/wiki/Shildon "Shildon"), [Spennymoor](/wiki/Spennymoor "Spennymoor"), [Stanley](/wiki/Stanley,_County_Durham "Stanley, County Durham") and [Tow Law](/wiki/Tow_Law "Tow Law").[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### 1836 to 1974

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 1836 to 1974")]

The county was aligned to other [historic counties of England](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") from 1836 until 1889; multiple acts were passed removing exclaves, splitting the county from the bishopric and reforming its structure.

The ceremonial county and [administrative county](/wiki/Administrative_county "Administrative county") were created under the [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888") in 1889. Darlington, Gateshead, West Hartlepool (later known as Hartlepool), South Shields and Sunderland became [county boroughs](/wiki/County_boroughs "County boroughs") during the administrative counties years of administrating; each remained in the ceremonial county while outside of the administrative county.

The ceremonial county remained under the same borders as the historic county until 1968 when the [County Borough of Teesside](/wiki/County_Borough_of_Teesside "County Borough of Teesside") formed. Ceremonial duties of the borough (which were made up of areas from two counties) were in the [North Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Riding_of_Yorkshire "North Riding of Yorkshire") ceremonial county.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

### 1974 to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 1974 to present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/England_Police_Forces_%28Durham%29.svg/250px-England_Police_Forces_%28Durham%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:England_Police_Forces_(Durham).svg)

Ceremonial county from 1974–1996

From the 1974 until 1996, the ceremonial county was split into eight [districts](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district"):[[43]](#cite_note-map-44)[[44]](#cite_note-darl1995-45)

* [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street_(district) "Chester-le-Street (district)"), including the Lumley, [Pelton](/wiki/Pelton,_County_Durham "Pelton, County Durham") and [Sacriston](/wiki/Sacriston "Sacriston") areas
* [Derwentside](/wiki/Derwentside "Derwentside"), including [Consett](/wiki/Consett "Consett") and [Stanley](/wiki/Stanley,_County_Durham "Stanley, County Durham")
* [City of Durham](/wiki/Durham_District "Durham District"), including [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") and the surrounding areas
* [Easington](/wiki/Easington_District "Easington District"), including [Seaham](/wiki/Seaham "Seaham") and [Peterlee](/wiki/Peterlee "Peterlee")
* [Borough of Sedgefield](/wiki/Sedgefield_(borough) "Sedgefield (borough)"), including [Spennymoor](/wiki/Spennymoor "Spennymoor"), [Sedgefield](/wiki/Sedgefield "Sedgefield") and [Newton Aycliffe](/wiki/Newton_Aycliffe "Newton Aycliffe")
* [Teesdale](/wiki/Teesdale_(district) "Teesdale (district)"), including [Barnard Castle](/wiki/Barnard_Castle "Barnard Castle") and the villages of Teesdale
* [Wear Valley](/wiki/Wear_Valley "Wear Valley"), including [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Bishop_Auckland "Bishop Auckland"), [Crook](/wiki/Crook,_County_Durham "Crook, County Durham"), [Willington](/wiki/Willington,_County_Durham "Willington, County Durham"), Hunwick, and the villages along [Weardale](/wiki/Weardale "Weardale")
* [Borough of Darlington](/wiki/Borough_of_Darlington "Borough of Darlington"), [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington") along with nearby villages around the town.

A [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") replaced the administrative county. The boundaries only deviated from the ceremonial boundaries after 1995 when the Darlington Borough became a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England").[[44]](#cite_note-darl1995-45)

On the 1 April 1996, the [county of Cleveland](/wiki/County_of_Cleveland "County of Cleveland") was abolished with its boroughs of [Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool") and [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees") (north of the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees")) becoming a part of the ceremonial county.[[5]](#cite_note-lieutenancies-6)[[45]](#cite_note-cleve-46)

The non-metropolitan county was reconstituted on 1 April 2009: the strategic services-providing [Durham County Council](/wiki/Durham_County_Council "Durham County Council") was re-organised into [a single district](/wiki/County_Durham_(district) "County Durham (district)") of the same name, merging with the seven local facility-providing districts in the non-metropolitan county and became structured as a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"). It had 126 councillors but since the 2025 local elections this was reduced to 98.[[46]](#cite_note-47) The three pre-existing unitary authorities were unaffected.

### Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Parliament")]

Main article: [List of Parliamentary constituencies in County Durham](/wiki/List_of_Parliamentary_constituencies_in_County_Durham "List of Parliamentary constituencies in County Durham")

The county boundaries used for parliamentary constituencies were originally those used between 1974 and 1996, consisting of the County Durham district and the Darlington Borough, until boundary changes in 2024. This area elects eight Members of Parliament. County Durham itself elects six of them, and as of the 2024 general election, all of these MPs are [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"). The rest of the ceremonial county is included in the [Cleveland parliamentary constituency area](/wiki/List_of_Parliamentary_constituencies_in_Cleveland "List of Parliamentary constituencies in Cleveland").

## Emergency services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Emergency services")]

The police and fire services operate according to the 1974-96 ceremonial county boundaries:

* [Durham Constabulary](/wiki/Durham_Constabulary "Durham Constabulary"),[[47]](#cite_note-police-48) [Ron Hogg](/wiki/Ron_Hogg "Ron Hogg") was first elected the [Durham Police and Crime Commissioner](/wiki/Durham_Police_and_Crime_Commissioner "Durham Police and Crime Commissioner") for the force on 15 November 2012, and held the position until his death in office in 2019.
* [Cleveland Police](/wiki/Cleveland_Police "Cleveland Police")
* [County Durham and Darlington Fire and Rescue Service](/wiki/County_Durham_and_Darlington_Fire_and_Rescue_Service "County Durham and Darlington Fire and Rescue Service"), it is under supervision of a combined fire authority consisting of 25 local councillors: 21 from Durham County Council and 4 from Darlington Borough Council.[[48]](#cite_note-49)
* [Cleveland Fire Brigade](/wiki/Cleveland_Fire_Brigade "Cleveland Fire Brigade").

The [North East Ambulance Service NHS Trust](/wiki/North_East_Ambulance_Service "North East Ambulance Service") cover [North East England](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England") and are responsible for providing ambulance services for the [NHS](/wiki/National_Health_Service_(England) "National Health Service (England)"). Northumbria Ambulance Service and County Durham Ambulance Service (following historic county borders) merged on 1 April 1999 to become the North East service.[[49]](#cite_note-50) In 2005 the area was adapted to the modern North East England regional extent.

Air ambulance services are provided by the [Great North Air Ambulance](/wiki/Great_North_Air_Ambulance "Great North Air Ambulance"). The charity operates three bases, including one in [Eaglescliffe](/wiki/Eaglescliffe "Eaglescliffe").

[Teesdale](/wiki/Teesdale "Teesdale") and [Weardale](/wiki/Weardale "Weardale") [Search and Mountain Rescue](/wiki/Mountain_rescue_in_England_and_Wales "Mountain rescue in England and Wales") Team, are based at Sniperly Farm in [Durham City](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") and respond to search and rescue incidents in the county.

### Employment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Employment")]

The proportion of the population working in agriculture fell from around 6% in 1851 to 1% in 1951; currently less than 1% of the population work in agriculture.[[21]](#cite_note-ns_durham-22) There were 15,202 people employed in coal mining in 1841, rising to a peak of 157,837 in 1921.[[21]](#cite_note-ns_durham-22)

County Durham has a mostly rural character, especially in the west. Small population centres scatter between Durham, Crook, Bishop Auckland, Shildon, Newton Aycliffe, Sedgefield and Peterlee. These places were populated by the draw of collieries during the Industrial Revolution. The south east side of the county Between Hartlepool and Darlington form part of [Teesside](/wiki/Teesside "Teesside") with settlements on the south side of the river.[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81) While the north east part of the county (on the border with Tyne and Wear) forms part of [Tyneside](/wiki/Tyneside "Tyneside") and [Wearside](/wiki/Wearside "Wearside").[[80]](#cite_note-82)

At the [2001 Census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Census_2001 "United Kingdom Census 2001"), Easington and Derwentside districts had the highest proportion (around 99%) in the county council area of resident population who were born in the UK.[[82]](#cite_note-ns_ethnic-84) 13.2% of the county council area's residents rate their health as *not good*, the highest proportion in England.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

96.6% of County Durham's residents are White British, with other white groups making up a further 1.6% of the population. Around 77% of the county's population are Christian whilst 22% have no religion, and around 1% come from other religious communities. These figures exclude around 6% of the population who did not wish to state their religion.

As at 2001, Chester-le-Street district has the lowest number of available jobs per working-age resident (0.38%).[[84]](#cite_note-ns_work-86)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Mining and heavy industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Mining and heavy industry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/John_Wilson_Carmichael_-_A_View_of_Murton_Colliery_near_Seaham%2C_County_Durham_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-John_Wilson_Carmichael_-_A_View_of_Murton_Colliery_near_Seaham%2C_County_Durham_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Wilson_Carmichael_-_A_View_of_Murton_Colliery_near_Seaham,_County_Durham_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

John Wilson Carmichael *A View of Murton Colliery near Seaham, County Durham*, 1843

A substantial number of colliery villages were built throughout the county in the nineteenth century to house the growing workforce, which included large numbers of migrant workers from the rest of the UK.[[85]](#cite_note-durhamrecordoffice.org.uk-87) Sometimes the migrants were brought in to augment the local workforce but, in other cases, they were brought in as strike breakers, or "blacklegs". Tens of thousands of people migrated to County Durham from Cornwall (partly due to their previous experience of [tin mining](/wiki/Tin_mining "Tin mining")) between 1815 and the outbreak of the First World War, so much so that the miners' cottages in east Durham called "Greenhill" were also known locally as "Cornwall", and Easington Colliery still has a Cornish Street.[[86]](#cite_note-88) Other migrants included people from Northumberland, Cumberland, South Wales, Scotland and Ireland.[[87]](#cite_note-co-curate.ncl.ac.uk-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90) Coal mining had a profound effect on trade unionism, public health and housing, as well as creating a related culture, language, folklore and sense of identity that still survives today.[[89]](#cite_note-durhamintime.org.uk-91)

The migrants also were employed in the railway, ship building, iron, steel and roadworking industries, and the pattern of migration continued, to a lesser extent, up until the 1950s and 1960s. Gateshead was once home to the fourth-largest Irish settlement in England,[[87]](#cite_note-co-curate.ncl.ac.uk-89) Consett's population was 22% Irish[[90]](#cite_note-92) and significant numbers of Irish people moved to Sunderland, resulting in the city hosting numerous events on [St. Patrick's Day](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Day "St. Patrick's Day") due to the Irish heritage.[[91]](#cite_note-93)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Durham_Miners_Gala_2008_Old_Elvet_Bridge.jpg/250px-Durham_Miners_Gala_2008_Old_Elvet_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Miners_Gala_2008_Old_Elvet_Bridge.jpg)

Durham Miners' Gala 2008

The culture of coal mining found expression in the [Durham Miners' Gala](/wiki/Durham_Miners%27_Gala "Durham Miners' Gala"), which was first held in 1871,[[92]](#cite_note-advice-94) developed around the culture of trade unionism. Coal mining continued to decline and pits closed. The [1984–1985 United Kingdom miners' strike](/wiki/1984%E2%80%931985_United_Kingdom_miners%27_strike "1984–1985 United Kingdom miners' strike") involved many miners across the county. Today no deep-coal mines exist in the county and numbers attending the Miners' Gala decreased over the period between the end of the strike and the 21st century. However recent years have seen numbers significantly grow, and more banners return to the Gala each year as former colliery communities restore or replicate former banners to march at the Gala parade.[[92]](#cite_note-advice-94)[[93]](#cite_note-heritage-95)

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Art")]

In 1930, the [Spennymoor Settlement](/wiki/Spennymoor_Settlement "Spennymoor Settlement") (otherwise known as the Pitman's Academy) opened. The settlement, initially funded by the [Pilgrim Trust](/wiki/Pilgrim_Trust "Pilgrim Trust"), aimed to encourage people to be neighbourly and participate in voluntary social service.[[94]](#cite_note-spennymoorsettlement.co.uk-96) The settlement operated during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), when unemployment was widespread and economic deprivation rife; Spennymoor was economically underprivileged. The settlement provided educational and social work, as well as hope; this included providing unemployed miners with on outlet for their creativity, a poor person's lawyer service, the town's first library and the Everyman Theatre. The output included paintings, sewing, socially-significant plays, woodwork and sculptures. Several members went on to win adult scholarships at Oxford University[[94]](#cite_note-spennymoorsettlement.co.uk-96) when such a route would normally be closed to the underprivileged. Former members include artists [Norman Cornish](/wiki/Norman_Cornish "Norman Cornish") and [Tom McGuinness](/wiki/Tom_McGuinness_(artist) "Tom McGuinness (artist)"), writer [Sid Chaplin](/wiki/Sid_Chaplin "Sid Chaplin") and journalist Arnold Hadwin (1929–2011). The Spennymoor Settlement at its home in the Everyman Theatre (Grade 2 listed) is still operating, administered by the current trustees, offering community events and activities, including Youth Theatre Group, an Art Group and various classes, as well as offering community accommodation facilities.

Several Durham miners have been able to turn their former mining careers into careers in art. For example, [Tom Lamb](/wiki/Tom_Lamb_(artist) "Tom Lamb (artist)"), as well as the aforementioned Tom McGuinness and Norman Cornish. Their artworks depict scenes of life underground, the streets in which they lived and the people they loved.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2017, The Mining Art Gallery opened in Bishop Auckland in a building that was once a bank.[[95]](#cite_note-97) Part of the Auckland Project, the gallery includes the work of artists from within County Durham and beyond, including such other North-Eastern mining artists as [Robert Olley](/wiki/Robert_Olley "Robert Olley"), as well as contributions from outside the region. It features three permanent areas and a temporary exhibition area; the gallery's Gemini Collection includes 420 pieces of mining art.
[[96]](#cite_note-98) Much of the artwork was donated, by Dr Robert McManners and Gillian Wales, for example.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

In 2019, 100 years after his birth, a permanent tribute to the work of the artist Norman Stansfield Cornish MBE was opened within the Town Hall, and a Cornish Trail around the town was established to include areas of the town depicted in Cornish's artwork.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Music")]

As with neighbouring Northumberland, County Durham has a rich heritage of [Northumbrian music](/wiki/Northumbrian_music "Northumbrian music"), dating back from the Northumbrian Golden Age of the 7th and 8th centuries. [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") made references to harp-playing, and abundant archeological evidence has been found of wooden flutes, bone flutes, panpipes, wooden drums and lyres (a six-string form of harp).[[9]](#cite_note-Gething-10) North-East England has a distinctive folk music style that has drawn from many other regions, including southern Scotland, Ireland and the rest of northern England, that has endured stably since the 18th century.[[98]](#cite_note-100) Instruments played include, in common with most folk music styles, stringed instruments such as the guitar and fiddle, but also the Northumbrian smallpipe, which is played and promoted by people including the [Northumbrian Pipers' Society](/wiki/Northumbrian_Pipers%27_Society "Northumbrian Pipers' Society") throughout the North East, including County Durham, with the society having an active group in Sedgefield.[[99]](#cite_note-101) Contemporary folk musicians include [Jez Lowe](/wiki/Jez_Lowe "Jez Lowe") and Ged Foley.

In 2018, The [Arts Council](/wiki/Arts_Council_England "Arts Council England") funded the Stories of Sanctuary project in the [city of Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"). The project aims to assist people living in the city to share their stories about seeking sanctuary in the North East through photography, stories, poetry and music. The art is based on a history of sanctuary in Durham, from St Cuthbert's exile, through to the miners' strike of 1984, and to refugees escaping civil war in the Middle East. The music produced as part of the project includes contributions from singer-songwriter Sam Slatcher and viola player Raghad Haddad from the National Syrian Orchestra.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

Other notable performers/songwriters who were born or raised in the county include [Paddy McAloon](/wiki/Paddy_McAloon "Paddy McAloon"), [Eric Boswell](/wiki/Eric_Boswell_(songwriter) "Eric Boswell (songwriter)"), [Jeremy Spencer](/wiki/Jeremy_Spencer "Jeremy Spencer"), [Alan Clark](/wiki/Alan_Clark_(keyboardist) "Alan Clark (keyboardist)"), [Martin Brammer](/wiki/Martin_Brammer "Martin Brammer"), [Robert Blamire](/wiki/Robert_Blamire "Robert Blamire"), [Thomas Allen](/wiki/Thomas_Allen_(baritone) "Thomas Allen (baritone)"), [Zoe Birkett](/wiki/Zoe_Birkett "Zoe Birkett"), [John O'Neill](/w/index.php?title=John_O%27Neill_(musician),_born_1926&action=edit&redlink=1 "John O'Neill (musician), born 1926 (page does not exist)"), [Karen Harding](/wiki/Karen_Harding "Karen Harding") and [Courtney Hadwin](/wiki/Courtney_Hadwin "Courtney Hadwin").

### Flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Flag")]

Main article: [Flag of County Durham](/wiki/Flag_of_County_Durham "Flag of County Durham")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Flag_of_County_Durham.svg/250px-Flag_of_County_Durham.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_County_Durham.svg)

County Durham flag

County Durham has an unofficial [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_County_Durham "Flag of County Durham"), registered with the [vexillological](/wiki/Vexillological "Vexillological") charity the [Flag Institute](/wiki/Flag_Institute "Flag Institute") on 21 November 2013.[[101]](#cite_note-103) The flag consists of St Cuthbert's cross counterchanged with the county's blue and gold colours.

Katie, Holly and James Moffatt designed the flag and entered their design into a competition launched by campaigner [Andy Strangeway](/wiki/Andy_Strangeway "Andy Strangeway"), who spoke of the flag as *"free, public symbol for all to use, especially on 20th March each year, which is not only County Durham Day but also St Cuthbert’s birthday”* (20 March is actually the date of Cuthbert's death).[[102]](#cite_note-104)[[103]](#cite_note-105)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Education")]

### University

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Durham&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: University")]

[Durham University](/wiki/Durham_University "Durham University") is based in [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") city and is sometimes held to be [the third oldest university in England](/wiki/Third-oldest_university_in_England_debate "Third-oldest university in England debate").[[104]](#cite_note-106) [Teesside University](/wiki/Teesside_University "Teesside University") has a campus in Darlington.
